Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Step-change upward in activity from 2020 to 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly totals jump sharply at the start of 2021 across transaction volume and business outcomes, indicating a clear level shift rather than a gradual rise.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Record count rises from 92 in 2020-12 to 711 in 2021-01. Total Sales rises from 8,026,527 in 2020-12 to 55,225,396 in 2021-01. Units Sold rises from 20,545.0 to 177,251.05 over the same months. Operating Profit rises from 2,712,198 to 19,311,961.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates that 2021 operates at a much larger monthly scale than 2020, so year-over-year comparisons should account for this structural shift.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2020 mid-year trough followed by partial recovery
Temporal Patterns.description: In 2020, business volume and outcomes decline into June, then recover in July through September, though not uniformly to earlier highs.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Total Sales falls from 24,607,006 in 2020-04 to 16,918,014 in 2020-05 and then to 8,829,819 in 2020-06, before recovering to 17,146,013 in 2020-07, 19,877,980 in 2020-08, and 18,304,436 in 2020-09. Units Sold drops from 45,123.595 in 2020-04 to 32,433.275 in 2020-05 and 16,269.0 in 2020-06, then rebounds to 37,220.0 in 2020-07, 56,992.32 in 2020-08, and 40,705.425 in 2020-09. Operating Profit similarly declines from 9,301,302 in 2020-04 to 2,292,728 in 2020-06, then rises to 4,917,674 in 2020-07, 7,338,929 in 2020-08, and 7,119,707 in 2020-09. Count also falls from 130 in 2020-04 to 60 in 2020-06, then recovers to 139 in 2020-08.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This shows a clear 2020 disruption period centered on June, followed by recovery, useful for identifying abnormal operating periods.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Late-2021 sales and profit acceleration after October dip
Temporal Patterns.description: After declining into October 2021, Total Sales and Operating Profit recover in November and rise strongly in December.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Total Sales declines from 72,288,221 in 2021-08 to 59,357,023 in 2021-09 and 53,074,764 in 2021-10, then rises to 59,235,040 in 2021-11 and 77,815,430 in 2021-12. Operating Profit follows the same pattern: 27,112,540 in 2021-08, 23,889,923 in 2021-09, 20,938,999 in 2021-10, then 21,972,071 in 2021-11 and 28,878,038 in 2021-12. Units Sold rises from 149,350.0 in 2021-11 to 170,172.075 in 2021-12, while mean Price per Unit rises from 50.213 in 2021-11 to 56.657 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates a pronounced year-end rebound in 2021, relevant for forecasting and planning around Q4 performance.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Price per Unit mean declines through early 2021, then climbs through year-end
Temporal Patterns.description: Average selling price falls from mid-2020 highs into early 2021, then trends upward from spring to December 2021.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Mean Price per Unit is 64.533 in 2020-06, then generally lower by early 2021: 38.221 in 2021-01, 36.633 in 2021-02, and 35.371 in 2021-03. It then increases across much of the rest of 2021: 38.322 in 2021-04, 45.110 in 2021-05, 48.148 in 2021-06, 51.960 in 2021-07, 50.213 in 2021-11, and 56.657 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This suggests pricing conditions were weaker in early 2021 and firmer later in the year, which matters for revenue mix and margin management.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold mean is higher in late 2020 than in most of 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average units per record peak in late summer 2020 and are generally lower across 2021.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Mean Units Sold reaches 410.017 in 2020-08 and 424.015 in 2020-09. In 2021, monthly means range from 209.937 in 2021-10 to 270.681 in 2021-08, with most months between roughly 213 and 266, all below the 2020-08 and 2020-09 levels.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates that larger 2021 totals are driven more by more records than by larger average order quantities.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 totals remain high despite relatively stable monthly counts
Temporal Patterns.description: Within 2021, transaction counts stay in a narrow band while sales and profit vary substantially month to month.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: In 2021, count stays between 662 and 728 each month. Over the same period, Total Sales ranges from 39,148,532 in 2021-03 to 78,334,681 in 2021-07 and 77,815,430 in 2021-12. Operating Profit ranges from 14,577,799 in 2021-03 to 29,137,256 in 2021-07 and 28,878,038 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Since record volume is comparatively stable in 2021, changes in revenue and profit likely align with per-record economics rather than major swings in transaction count.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Mean Total Sales per record weakens from 2020 peaks into early 2021, then recovers mid-to-late 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average revenue per record is high in parts of 2020, falls to a low in early 2021, and then improves again by mid and late 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This shows that per-transaction revenue compressed sharply at the start of 2021 before improving later, which is important for understanding order quality over time.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Mean Operating Profit per record follows a similar dip-and-recovery pattern
Temporal Patterns.description: Average operating profit per record is stronger in parts of 2020, lower in early 2021, and then improves later in 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates profitability per record was compressed in early 2021 and improved later, relevant for margin and operating efficiency analysis.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: No clear repeated month-of-year seasonality across both years
Temporal Patterns.description: The same calendar months do not show consistent highs or lows in both 2020 and 2021, so repeated seasonality is not clearly supported by the two-year monthly aggregates.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: For example, Total Sales is lowest in 2020-12 at 8,026,527 among late-2020 months, but 2021-12 is one of the highest months at 77,815,430. August is strong in both years for Units Sold totals (56,992.32 in 2020-08 and 193,266.15 in 2021-08), but April is a peak month in 2020 Total Sales at 24,607,006 while 2021 peaks occur later, such as 78,334,681 in 2021-07 and 77,815,430 in 2021-12.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This cautions against assuming stable calendar seasonality from these two years alone; structural changes appear stronger than recurring seasonal effects.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales and Operating Profit move together very strongly
Correlation Patterns.description: Total Sales and Operating Profit have a very strong positive relationship. Records with higher gross sales tend to also show higher operating profit. This is likely largely structural because operating profit is financially downstream from sales, though the exact profit level also depends on costs and margin.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Strong positive correlation, r = 0.9563. As Total Sales increases, Operating Profit also increases strongly.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates profit is tightly linked to revenue generation in this dataset. Sales growth is closely associated with profit growth, so revenue-driving actions are likely to have a direct effect on operating profit.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold is strongly associated with Total Sales
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Total Sales show a strong positive relationship. Records with more units sold generally produce higher total sales. This relationship is partly structural because sales revenue depends on quantity sold and price per unit.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Strong positive correlation, r = 0.8449. Higher Units Sold co-moves with higher Total Sales.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: Volume sold is a major driver of revenue in the dataset. Increasing unit volume appears strongly connected with higher sales outcomes.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold is strongly associated with Operating Profit
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Operating Profit have a strong positive relationship. Records with higher sales volume tend to generate higher operating profit. This is likely an indirect structural relationship because more units sold raises sales, which in turn is strongly tied to profit.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Strong positive correlation, r = 0.8139. As Units Sold increases, Operating Profit tends to increase as well.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: Sales volume is strongly linked not just to revenue but also to profitability. Efforts that increase unit movement may also improve operating profit, assuming margins are maintained.

Grouping Patterns.description: A small set of retailers accounts for much larger record counts than the rest, indicating an uneven distribution by retailer segment.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Retailer counts are highest for Foot Locker (2,637), West Gear (2,374), and Sports Direct (2,032). These are far above Walmart (626), Amazon (949), and Kohl's (1,030). Foot Locker has 4.21× as many records as Walmart (2,637 vs 626) and 2.78× as many as Amazon (2,637 vs 949).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Any retailer-level performance summary will be heavily influenced by Foot Locker, West Gear, and Sports Direct because they represent the largest portions of the dataset.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer leaders in total units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: Total units sold are concentrated among three retailers, with West Gear leading overall.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold by retailer: West Gear 619,822.445, Foot Locker 569,453.95, Sports Direct 553,494.15, Kohl's 287,375.0, Amazon 197,990.0, Walmart 193,412.825. West Gear sells 3.20× Walmart's total units (619,822.445 vs 193,412.825) and 2.15× Amazon's (619,822.445 vs 197,990.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Volume is dominated by West Gear, Foot Locker, and Sports Direct, so inventory, partnerships, and forecasting decisions should prioritize these retailers.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Walmart has the highest average units per record despite low total volume
Grouping Patterns.description: Walmart has the highest average units sold per record, even though it has the lowest record count and one of the lowest total unit sums.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Average units sold per retailer: Walmart 308.97, Kohl's 279.00, Sports Direct 272.39, West Gear 261.09, Foot Locker 215.95, Amazon 208.63. Walmart's mean is 1.48× Amazon's (308.97 vs 208.63), but Walmart has only 626 records versus Amazon's 949 and total units of 193,412.825 versus Amazon's 197,990.0.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Walmart appears to generate larger order sizes per record, which may matter for channel strategy, fulfillment planning, or account management.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Amazon has the highest average price per unit among retailers
Grouping Patterns.description: Average selling price differs across retailers, with Amazon at the top and Sports Direct at the bottom.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by retailer: Amazon 48.76, Walmart 47.18, West Gear 46.74, Foot Locker 44.78, Kohl's 44.61, Sports Direct 42.05. Amazon's average price is 6.71 higher than Sports Direct's (48.76 vs 42.05), about 1.16× as high.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate. Retailers are not only different in volume but also in pricing level, which can affect margin management and retailer positioning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: West and Northeast dominate regional record counts
Grouping Patterns.description: Regional distribution is uneven, with West and Northeast carrying the largest number of records.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Regional counts: West 2,448, Northeast 2,376, Midwest 1,872, South 1,728, Southeast 1,224. West has exactly 2.00× the Southeast's records (2,448 vs 1,224).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Regional analyses will be weighted toward West and Northeast due to their larger representation in the data.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: West leads regional total units sold, but Southeast leads average units per record
Grouping Patterns.description: The region with the most total units is not the same as the region with the largest average order size.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total regional units sold: West 684,912.075, South 489,063.2, Northeast 471,618.69, Midwest 391,337.0, Southeast 384,617.405. Mean units sold: Southeast 314.23, South 283.02, West 279.78, Midwest 209.05, Northeast 198.49. West leads total volume, but Southeast has the highest average units per record at 314.23, which is 1.58× Northeast's 198.49.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. West is the largest volume region overall, while Southeast appears to have larger transactions per record, suggesting different operational and sales strategies by region.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Southeast and West are the highest-price regions
Grouping Patterns.description: Regional pricing is uneven, with Southeast and West clearly above South and Midwest.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by region: Southeast 50.18, West 49.94, Northeast 46.69, Midwest 40.36, South 38.24. Southeast's mean price is 11.95 higher than South's (50.18 vs 38.24), about 1.31× as high.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Pricing differs materially by region, which is relevant for regional pricing strategy and demand planning.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: A few states dominate total units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: State-level unit volume is concentrated in a small number of states, especially California, Texas, and New York.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Top state totals for units sold are California 162,260.025, Texas 156,303.2, New York 139,479.69, and Florida 122,700.74. California sells 8.47× Nebraska's total units (162,260.025 vs 19,154.0) and 5.84× Pennsylvania's (162,260.025 vs 27,662.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. A small set of states contributes disproportionately to unit volume, making them priority markets for inventory and sales focus.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Large spread in state-level average units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: Average units sold per record vary widely across states, showing strong segmentation in transaction size.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Highest mean units sold by state include South Carolina 452.27, North Carolina 422.66, New York 387.44, California 375.60, and Texas 361.81. Lowest include Rhode Island 127.19, Pennsylvania 128.06, Nebraska 133.01, Maryland 144.57, and Minnesota 144.71. South Carolina's mean is 3.56× Rhode Island's (452.27 vs 127.19).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Transaction size differs substantially by state, which can affect staffing, replenishment, and local sales tactics.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: State-level price per unit is highly uneven
Grouping Patterns.description: Average price per unit varies sharply across states, with some states priced much higher than others.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Highest mean prices by state are Washington 67.65, Hawaii 64.25, Orlando's state Florida city-level is high but at state level Florida is 56.69, Colorado 60.09, and Alaska 57.22. Lowest state means include Utah 26.83, Missouri 31.49, Arkansas 33.34, Oklahoma 33.42, and Mississippi 35.48. Washington's mean price is 2.52× Utah's (67.65 vs 26.83).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. The pricing spread is large enough to matter for regional pricing policy, promotion design, and margin expectations.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Record counts at state level follow a tiered structure
Grouping Patterns.description: State counts are not evenly distributed; they cluster into a few discrete count tiers.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: State record counts appear in repeated tiers: 144 for many states (e.g., Illinois, Indiana, Iowa), 216 for many states (e.g., Alabama, Arizona, Georgia), 360 for Florida and New York, and 432 for California and Texas. California and Texas each have 3.00× the records of a 144-count state (432 vs 144).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate. The dataset has uneven state representation, so comparisons across states should account for much larger sample sizes in California and Texas than in many other states.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: City-level volume is concentrated in a few large markets
Grouping Patterns.description: A small number of cities account for the highest total units sold, while many cities are far lower.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Top city totals for units sold include Charleston 95,000.49, Houston 87,125.2, San Francisco 85,876.025, New York 82,293.69, and Los Angeles 76,384.0. Charleston sells 4.96× Omaha's total units (95,000.49 vs 19,154.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Sales volume is concentrated in a limited set of cities, which supports prioritizing those markets for local inventory and promotion efforts.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Very large differences in city-level average units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: Average units sold per record differ sharply across cities, indicating strong segmentation in order size by market.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Highest city mean units sold include Miami 433.37, Charlotte 422.66, Houston 403.36, San Francisco 397.57, and Albany 397.13. Lowest include Providence 127.19, Philadelphia 128.06, Omaha 133.01, Baltimore 144.57, and Minneapolis 144.71. Miami's mean is 3.41× Providence's (433.37 vs 127.19).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Market-level differences in transaction size are substantial and could influence assortment, staffing, and fulfillment decisions.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Product counts are nearly balanced across categories
Grouping Patterns.description: Unlike retailer and region distributions, product categories are represented very evenly in record count.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Product counts are tightly grouped: Men's Apparel 1,606, Men's Athletic Footwear 1,610, Men's Street Footwear 1,610, Women's Apparel 1,608, Women's Athletic Footwear 1,606, Women's Street Footwear 1,608. The maximum difference is only 4 records (1,610 vs 1,606).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: Moderate. Product-level comparisons are less likely to be distorted by sample-size imbalance because category representation is nearly equal.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Apparel categories have higher average prices than footwear categories
Grouping Patterns.description: Average price per unit is consistently higher for apparel than for footwear, especially compared with women's footwear categories.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit: Women's Apparel 51.60, Men's Apparel 50.32, Men's Street Footwear 44.24, Men's Athletic Footwear 43.78, Women's Athletic Footwear 41.11, Women's Street Footwear 40.25. Women's Apparel is 11.35 higher than Women's Street Footwear (51.60 vs 40.25), about 1.28× as high.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Product mix matters for pricing strategy because apparel categories command materially higher unit prices than footwear categories.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Men's Street Footwear dominates product unit volume
Grouping Patterns.description: Among products, Men's Street Footwear is the clear leader in both total units sold and average units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold by product: Men's Street Footwear 555,462.895, Men's Athletic Footwear 429,482.05, Women's Apparel 423,612.225, Women's Street Footwear 389,072.2, Women's Athletic Footwear 317,236.0, Men's Apparel 306,683.0. Mean units sold: Men's Street Footwear 345.01, Men's Athletic Footwear 266.76, Women's Apparel 263.44, Women's Street Footwear 241.96, Women's Athletic Footwear 197.53, Men's Apparel 190.96. Men's Street Footwear total is 1.81× Men's Apparel (555,462.895 vs 306,683.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Men's Street Footwear is the dominant product segment by volume and should be central to demand planning and assortment decisions.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Women's Apparel combines the highest product price with high unit volume
Grouping Patterns.description: Women's Apparel stands out as the highest-priced product category while also maintaining high total and average unit sales relative to most other categories.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Women's Apparel has the highest mean price per unit at 51.60. Its total units sold are 423,612.225 and mean units sold are 263.44, both above Men's Apparel (306,683.0 total; 190.96 mean), Women's Athletic Footwear (317,236.0 total; 197.53 mean), and Women's Street Footwear in price (40.25 mean price). Only Men's Street Footwear and Men's Athletic Footwear exceed it in total or mean units.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: High. Women's Apparel appears attractive because it combines premium pricing with relatively strong volume, making it important for revenue and margin planning.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: High-end price outliers above expected range
Anomaly Patterns.description: A small set of records have unusually high unit prices relative to the central price distribution.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Price per Unit has 81 outliers (0.84%) using bounds of 5.0 to 85.0. The observed maximum is 110.0, while the median is 45.0 and q75 is 55.0. The mean is 45.22, close to the median, and skewness is only 0.36, so the anomaly is concentrated in a small upper tail rather than across the full distribution.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Likely a mix of legitimate premium pricing and possible pricing-entry exceptions. Keep these records, but validate values above 85 before modeling price sensitivity; consider winsorizing or using robust methods if price-driven models are sensitive to tail values.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Upper-tail volume spikes in units sold
Anomaly Patterns.description: Units Sold shows a pronounced right tail with a nontrivial number of unusually large transactions.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Units Sold has 627 outliers (6.50%) above the upper bound of 655.0; the maximum is 975.0. The mean is 250.99 versus a median of 175.0, with q75 at 325.0, skewness of 1.36, and kurtosis of 1.16. These statistics indicate large-volume spikes pulling the average upward.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: More likely real business events such as bulk orders or promotion-driven spikes than random noise, because the tail is substantial and aligned with positive skew. Retain for revenue analysis, but use robust summaries, segment large orders separately, and test model sensitivity with and without high-volume records.

Anomaly Patterns.description: Total Sales contains very large revenue spikes relative to the typical transaction size, producing a highly uneven distribution.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Total Sales has 653 outliers (6.77%) above the upper bound of 368618.25, with a maximum of 825000.0. The mean is 93273.44 while the median is only 9576.0, a very large gap. q25 is 4254.5 and q75 is 150000.0, with skewness 1.84 and kurtosis 3.14. This shows a heavy upper tail and concentration of very large sales records.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: This pattern is consistent with a mix of many small transactions and a smaller number of very large deals. Likely mostly real business variation, but values near the maximum should be validated because they strongly influence averages. Use log transforms, medians, or trimmed means in downstream analysis.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Highly concentrated profit in a small set of records
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Profit is even more extreme than Total Sales, with very large positive profit spikes and a heavy upper tail.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Operating Profit has 706 outliers (7.32%) above the upper bound of 127274.5, the highest outlier share among the measured business variables. The maximum is 390000.0. The mean is 34425.28 versus a median of 4371.5, with q25 at 1922.0 and q75 at 52063.0. Skewness is 2.23 and kurtosis is 5.82, indicating a very heavy-tailed distribution dominated by extreme positive values.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Likely reflects a small number of very profitable transactions rather than symmetric noise. These records are important for profitability analysis but can distort averages and model coefficients. Keep them, validate the largest profits, and prefer robust or transformed modeling approaches.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Possible zero-inflation in sales and profit measures
Anomaly Patterns.description: Both Total Sales and Operating Profit have minimum values of zero, suggesting a cluster at zero may exist, which would be unusual for completed sale records if frequent.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: The minimum is 0.0 for Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold. At the same time, q25 values are positive: 4254.5 for Total Sales, 1922.0 for Operating Profit, and 105.0 for Units Sold. This means zeros occur but are not common enough to pull the 25th percentile to zero. Exact zero counts are not provided, so the extent of zero-inflation cannot be quantified from the available statistics.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Zeros could be legitimate canceled, returned, or placeholder records, or they could indicate data quality issues if these are supposed to be completed sales. Downstream, quantify zero counts before modeling; if material, treat zero records as a separate process or filter them depending on business rules.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Margin values outside the main operating range
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Margin is generally stable but includes a small number of unusually low or high percentage values.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Operating Margin has 44 outliers (0.46%) outside bounds of 14.0 to 70.0. The observed range is 10.0 to 80.0, while the median is 41.0, q25 is 35.0, q75 is 49.0, and the mean is 42.30. Skewness (0.23) and kurtosis (0.18) are low, so the distribution is otherwise fairly regular.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: These are isolated exceptions rather than a broad distribution problem. They may reflect unusual promotions, cost allocations, or occasional data-entry issues. Keep but review margins below 14 or above 70; cap or exclude only if business validation shows they are invalid.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer ID concentration at a single value
Anomaly Patterns.description: Retailer ID appears unusually concentrated, with both the 25th and 75th percentiles equal to the same value.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Retailer ID has q25 = 1185732.0, median = 1185732.0, and q75 = 1185732.0, meaning at least the middle 50% of records share the same ID value. The mean is 1173849.72, min is 1128299.0, max is 1197831.0, and skewness is -1.08, showing the distribution is heavily concentrated rather than spread across many IDs.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: As an identifier, this is not a numeric outlier issue but it is an unusual distribution pattern that may indicate one retailer dominates the dataset or that IDs are not being used at transaction-level granularity. Treat Retailer ID as categorical only; check entity counts and class imbalance before retailer-level analysis.
